title: bangkok fine dining restaurants
full_text: Main Eat Drink Reviews Insider At Home About Us Download Our Magazine Q2 2026 Q1 2026 Vol 1 Palate Dining Guide 2025-2026 Main Eat Drink Reviews Insider At Home About Us Download Our Magazine Q2 2026 Q1 2026 Vol 1 Palate Dining Guide 2025-2026 Now Reading: Where to Eat in Bangkok Now: The Restaurants Redefining Fine Dining 1 01 Where to Eat in Bangkok Now: The Restaurants Redefining Fine Dining Main// Eat// Drink// Reviews// Insider// At Home// About Us// Download Our Magazine// Q2 2026// Q1 2026// Vol 1 Palate Dining Guide 2025-2026// Home Eat Where to Eat in Bangkok Now: The Restaurants Redefining Fine DiningWhere to Eat in Bangkok Now: The Restaurants Redefining Fine DiningAndrea WongEat1 month agoShare Spanning cuisines and styles, Bangkok’s most compelling kitchens aren’t chasing the next thing. They are the next thing—and this is where to find them. Bangkok’s fine-dining landscape continues to evolve, shaped by chefs increasingly focused on refinement over reinvention. Less a definitive ranking than a guide by inclination, what follows spans cuisines and approaches that may come as a surprise—reflecting where the city is headed next. Reimagining Thai royal cuisine Dry Aged Fish with Tom Kha Cold Soup.Photo: Courtesy of WisetWiso. A recent addition to Bangkok’s dining scene, WisetWiso opens with a name steeped in history—wiset for the cooks of royal courts, wiso for beauty—and the kitchen makes good on both.Chef Chakkrapoom Boonyakom grew up inside Thai culinary tradition in the most literal sense: a family with royal cooking roots, a grandmother who served in the palace. That inheritance shows—not as nostalgia, but as conviction. The cooking is precise without being precious, rooted without being rigid, and sourced with the kind of care that makes seasonality feel less like a buzzword and more like a philosophy.Highlights from the tasting menu linger: a chilled tom kha poured over dry-aged fish, cool and composed; a pomelo salad with sun-dried squid roe that brings a savoury edge; a rich and grounding beef panang with kra-bok rice. The glutinous rice dumpling in spiced ginger soup is a fitting close, warm and slow to leave. IG: @wisetwisobkk Familiar Thai flavours, elevated with intent Cassava noodles layered with freshwater algae, bean sprouts and pine pepper.Photo: Courtesy of Khaan. Khaan’s tasting menu begins with a simple premise—Thai street food, taken seriously—and builds from there. The kitchen operates on a zero-waste philosophy, sourcing directly from farmers and fishermen across Rayong and Ranong, using ingredients end to end: seafood trimmings fermented into sauces, fruit peels reworked into infusions. Nothing is incidental.Familiar formats are the starting point, not the destination. A cassava noodle dish is built on roasted vegetable and fruit scrap sauces, finished with crispy freshwater algae, house-grown bean sprouts and pine pepper—diners season it themselves in a nod to street-side dining. A crocodile tongue stir-fry sharpened with herbaceous pad cha makes the case for the unfamiliar: textural, punchy, and gone before you second-guess it.Each course comes with just enough context—provenance, inspiration—without overexplaining. Pairings in wine, tea, or juice thread through the progression, lifting aromatics and softening edges without pulling focus. IG: khaan.bkk A new standard in refined Chinese dining The interior is reminiscent of a modern Jiangnan courtyard.Photo: Courtesy of Purple Laurel. Purple Laurel arrives in Bangkok with a clear pedigree: the first overseas outpost of celebrated Chinese chef Yu Bin, awarded a Diamond by China’s Black Pearl Guide and named Best Chinese Restaurant in Bangkok by BK Top Tables this year.The kitchen draws from Jiangnan cuisine—the delicate, ingredient-driven cooking of China’s eastern heartland—layered with modern Cantonese technique. Chef Yu Bin’s “Yu-style” philosophy runs through every dish: precise, globally inflected, and grounded in his Hangzhou origins without being confined by them. The space at Gaysorn Amarin follows suit, unfolding like a modern Jiangnan courtyard, where lattice carvings, hand-painted wallpaper, and lush greenery create an atmosphere that feels both intimate and transportive.The menu has its standouts: chilled cherry jelly with foie gras; tiger grouper steamed with yellow pepper sauce; a traditional Peking duck that earns its place on any list. A koi fish pudding with passion fruit closes things on a bright, clean note. IG: @purplelaurel.bkk Indian cuisine beyond the expected Kundapura Ghee Roast Chicken with Kundapur Masala and Cripsy Cone Dosa.Photo: Courtesy of Jhol. Jhol has spent the past six years reshaping what Indian food looks like in Bangkok, moving the conversation away from the familiar and into the full breadth of the country’s coastline. Think less butter chicken, more the layered, spice-forward cooking of regions stretching from West Bengal to Gujarat, where seafood, coconut, and tropical ingredients like tamarind and kokum take the lead.The menu is punchy and deeply satisfying. The masala muska bun is buttery, spiced, and hard to stop at one; the Berhampur fried chicken wings come tangy and bold with just the right hit of heat; and the Andhra biryani lands fragrant and full-bodied.Drinks here never feel like an afterthought. Cocktails mirror the food’s intensity—bright, spiced, occasionally smoky—built to move with the food, not against it. The mocktails are just as impressive: the Dushala, a mix of berries, roselle, and jasmine finished with an orange sphere and popping candy, is as playful as it sounds and better than it needs to be.
